papers.title: Signatures in the gut microbiome of German elite athletes: insights from a matched-subgroup analysis.
papers.abstract: Elite athletes undergo intense physical training and experience substantial physiological stress, which could affect the composition and function of their gut microbiome. This study compared the gut microbiomes of 148 German junior and senior elite athletes with those of 108 healthy adults to identify taxonomic and functional features associated with elite athletic status. Group comparisons were conducted between healthy adults, senior athletes, and junior athletes, and a matched-subgroup analysis was performed in adults only, controlling for age, sex, body mass index, and dietary pattern. Sig
papers.summary: This study compared the gut microbiomes of 148 German elite athletes with 108 healthy adults, finding taxonomic and functional features associated with elite athletic status. A matched-subgroup analysis controlling for age, sex, BMI, and diet revealed specific microbial signatures linked to intense physical training.

papers.title: Uncovering transcriptional processes in microbial communities adapted to differing saline conditions in salt-weathered historic buildings.
papers.abstract: Microbial colonization of architectural surfaces in historic buildings can cause not only aesthetic damage but also biodeterioration. One example is the colonizing microbiome on salt-weathered architectural surfaces. Halotolerant and halophilic communities on such surfaces produce colored pigments that visually alter cultural heritage sites and could potentially degrade organic binders used for mural paintings. Although the microorganisms involved in these deterioration processes have already been described, detailed information about the molecular processes that allow these communities to suc
papers.summary: This study investigates the transcriptional processes of microbial communities living on salt-weathered historic buildings, revealing how different salt conditions shape gene expression related to stress tolerance and pigment production. The findings provide molecular insights into how these halotolerant and halophilic communities survive and cause biodeterioration, which could inform conservation strategies for cultural heritage sites.

papers.title: Kente: A Graph-based Pangenomic Approach for Horizontal Gene Transfer Detection in Microbiomes.
papers.abstract: Horizontal gene transfer (HGT) shapes bacterial evolution and microbial ecosystems, yet detecting HGT within microbiomes remains a challenge due to fragmented metagenomic assemblies, reference bias, reliance on gene boundaries, and limited ability to model structural mosaicism and patterns across genomes.
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: Kente is a new graph-based pangenomic method that detects horizontal gene transfer in microbiomes by modeling structural mosaicism across genomes, overcoming limitations of fragmented assemblies and reference bias. It enables more accurate identification of HGT events in complex microbial communities.

papers.title: Quantitative detection of gut microbial eukaryotes with EukDetect2 reveals global distribution of commensal protists and association with distinct microbial community structure.
papers.abstract: Microbial eukaryotes are prevalent members of host-associated and free-living microbial communities, but are routinely excluded from studies of these communities. Existing methods for eukaryote detection from whole metagenome sequencing are limited by contamination of eukaryotic reference genomes and incomplete taxonomic coverage. Our previously published tool EukDetect addressed these challenges using a curated database of universal BUSCO marker genes, but lacked validated quantitative abundance metrics and was built from a limited number of genomes. Here we present EukDetect2, incorporating
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: EukDetect2 is an updated computational tool that accurately detects and quantifies microbial eukaryotes (like protists) in metagenomic sequencing data, overcoming previous limitations of contamination and incomplete reference genomes. Using this tool, the authors discovered that commensal protists are globally distributed in the human gut and are associated with distinct bacterial community structures. This work provides a valuable new resource for studying the overlooked eukaryotic members of the gut microbiome.

papers.title: Detoxifying and depolymerizing microorganisms reveal intertwined guild collaborations in the gut microbiome of the generalist macro-algivorous fish Kyphosus cinerascens.
papers.abstract: The biotransformation of macroalgal biomass represents a major catabolic challenge due to its structurally diverse polysaccharides and inhibitory polyphenols. Unlike terrestrial lignocellulosic substrates, macroalgal polysaccharides contain multiple monomer types, branching patterns, and sulfation states. Additionally, toxic macroalgal polyphenols have been shown to inhibit both microbial growth and their catalytic enzymes. While herbivorous fishes have evolved specialized gut microbiota to process these substrates, the enzymatic pathways remain poorly characterized, with few experimentally va
papers.summary: This study reveals that the gut microbiome of the herbivorous fish Kyphosus cinerascens relies on collaborative guilds of microorganisms to detoxify and depolymerize macroalgal biomass. The microbes work together to break down structurally complex polysaccharides and neutralize inhibitory polyphenols, highlighting a symbiotic network that enables efficient digestion of macroalgae.

papers.title: Gut barrier dysfunction and multidrug-resistant bacterial translocation in adult critical illness: Mechanistic insights from a systematic review.
papers.abstract: The gastrointestinal tract plays an important role in host defence during critical illness. Disruption of epithelial integrity, microbiome imbalance, and immune dysregulation have all been linked to the translocation of multidrug-resistant (MDR) organisms from intestinal colonization to invasive infection. However, whether these associations reflect true causal mechanisms remains uncertain, and available human evidence has not been comprehensively synthesized using current methodological standards.
papers.summary: This systematic review examines how gut barrier dysfunction, microbiome imbalance, and immune dysregulation may allow multidrug-resistant bacteria to move from the gut into the bloodstream in critically ill adults. The authors find that while these factors are associated with bacterial translocation, current human evidence is insufficient to establish clear causal mechanisms. The review highlights the need for more rigorous studies to understand and prevent these dangerous infections.

papers.title: Letter to the Editor: Circadian and microbial misalignment in metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease - mechanistic insights and chronotherapeutic potential.
papers.abstract: We read with interest the article by Rusman et al published in World Journal of Experimental Medicine. Beyond microbial composition, the gut-liver axis is a rhythmic system regulated by a bidirectional interaction between host clocks and the gut microbiota. Social jetlag induces "temporal dysbiosis", disrupting the timing of metabolites and compromising the intestinal barrier, which exacerbates metabolic injury in metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease. Restoration of these rhythms through chronotherapeutic approaches provides an effective method to restore alignment and impr
papers.summary: This letter discusses how social jetlag disrupts the timing of gut microbiota and host circadian rhythms, leading to 'temporal dysbiosis' that worsens metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease. The authors propose that chronotherapeutic approaches could restore alignment between circadian and microbial rhythms to improve liver health.

papers.title: Soil Resistomes in a Tropical Watershed are Indirectly Structured by Bacterial Community Interactions with Soil Properties.
papers.abstract: Soils are recognized as reservoirs of antibiotic resistance genes (ARGs) with the potential to transfer to clinical pathogens, creating antimicrobial resistance (AMR) that poses a threat to human health. While large-scale AMR surveys have profiled how diverse biomes shape soil resistomes, less is known about the influence of specific soil properties. Here, we combined metagenomics and 16S rRNA amplicon sequencing with isolate-based approaches to investigate drivers of soil AMR across a tropical watershed from beach to mountaintop in Waimea Valley, O'ahu, Hawai i. We leveraged functional- and t
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: This study investigates how soil properties and bacterial community interactions shape antibiotic resistance genes (ARGs) in a tropical watershed, finding that soil resistomes are indirectly structured by bacterial community composition rather than directly by soil properties. Using metagenomics and 16S rRNA sequencing across diverse habitats from beach to mountaintop, the authors show that soil properties influence bacterial communities, which in turn determine ARG profiles. This work provides insights into the ecological drivers of antimicrobial resistance in tropical soils.

papers.title: Eco-evolutionary dynamics of defense systems in mobile genetic elements: Cui bono?
papers.abstract: Mobile genetic elements (MGEs), including viruses, plasmids, and transposons, are major drivers of evolution in bacteria and archaea. Host-parasite conflicts drive the emergence of a broad variety of defense and counter-defense systems. Recent advances in metagenomics and functional annotation have shown that many defense systems are located on MGEs. The fact that MGEs are, essentially, genomic parasites raises an intriguing question: why do these parasites carry defense systems at high prevalence, often even higher than the host chromosome?
papers.summary: This paper explores why mobile genetic elements (MGEs) like viruses and plasmids frequently carry defense systems, often at higher prevalence than host chromosomes. The authors propose eco-evolutionary dynamics where these systems benefit the MGEs themselves by protecting their own genomes or by manipulating host competition, challenging the view that MGEs are purely parasitic. The work synthesizes recent metagenomic findings to reframe the evolutionary roles of defense systems in microbial communities.

papers.title: AI-Accelerated Structure Elucidation of Boavistamides A-C, Cyclic Depsipeptides from a Marine Filamentous Cyanobacterium Collected in Cabo Verde.
papers.abstract: Boavistamide A (1), a new alkyne-containing cyclic depsipeptide featuring the rare 3-amino-2-methyl-7-octynoic acid (AMOYA) moiety, was discovered along with two structurally related analogs, boavistamides B and C (2 and 3), from a filamentous marine cyanobacterium collected on Boa Vista Island, Cabo Verde. Their isolation was guided by antiplasmodial activity, GNPS MS/MS molecular networking, LC-MS profiling, and dereplication using the MarinLit database. The planar structures of boavistamides A-C (1-3) were elucidated through comprehensive HRMS and 1D/2D NMR analyses, with annotation support
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: Researchers discovered three new cyclic depsipeptides (boavistamides A-C) from a marine cyanobacterium collected in Cabo Verde, using AI-assisted methods like GNPS molecular networking and database dereplication. The compounds, one containing a rare amino acid, were structurally characterized by mass spectrometry and NMR, and showed antiplasmodial activity. This demonstrates how AI can accelerate natural product discovery from marine microbes.

papers.title: CAMBRA caries risk stratification is associated with distinct salivary and supragingival plaque microbiomes in pre-orthodontic patients.
papers.abstract: Fixed orthodontic appliances impair oral hygiene and increase the risk of dental caries and white spot lesions (WSLs). Although the Caries Management by Risk Assessment (CAMBRA) tool enables multifactorial caries risk evaluation, its association with the oral microbiome remains unclear. In this cross-sectional study, we examined the relationship between CAMBRA risk classification, clinical indices, and salivary and supragingival plaque microbiomes in 149 pre-orthodontic patients. Participants were classified into Low, Moderate, High, and Extreme risk groups based on CAMBRA. We evaluated the de
papers.summary: This study found that CAMBRA caries risk stratification in pre-orthodontic patients is associated with distinct microbial communities in saliva and supragingival plaque, with higher risk groups showing different bacterial compositions. The findings suggest that CAMBRA risk categories reflect underlying microbiome differences, which could inform personalized preventive strategies for orthodontic patients.

papers.title: Association between salivary microbiota-related amino acid metabolic dysregulation and tacrolimus-induced gingival overgrowth following kidney transplantation.
papers.abstract: Kidney transplant (KT) recipients require lifelong immunosuppressive therapy to prevent allograft rejection. Drug-induced gingival overgrowth (DIGO) is a notable adverse effect of tacrolimus, for which effective preventive or therapeutic strategies are lacking. Dysbiosis of the oral microbiota has been implicated as a major risk factor for DIGO. However, its mechanistic role remains poorly understood.
papers.summary: This study investigates how changes in the oral microbiome, specifically disruptions in amino acid metabolism, may contribute to gum overgrowth caused by the immunosuppressant drug tacrolimus in kidney transplant patients. By analyzing salivary microbiota, the researchers identified metabolic dysregulation linked to this side effect, offering potential targets for prevention or treatment. The findings highlight a mechanistic connection between oral microbial imbalance and drug-induced gingival overgrowth.

papers.title: Pre-treatment Gut Microbiome Diversity and Function Linked to Cytotoxic and Natural Killer Cell Immune Responses after N-803 Treatment in People with HIV.
papers.abstract: N-803, an IL-15 superagonist, is currently being studied in clinical trials as a treatment to reverse HIV latency. However, its effects on the gut microbiome are not well understood.
papers.journal: Clinical infectious diseases : an official publication of the Infectious Diseases Society of America
papers.summary: This study found that the diversity and function of the gut microbiome before treatment with N-803 (an IL-15 superagonist) are linked to immune responses involving cytotoxic and natural killer cells in people with HIV. The findings suggest that the gut microbiome may influence the effectiveness of this latency-reversing treatment.

papers.title: Gut microbiome dynamics and alcohol use outcomes during naltrexone treatment: a 12 week follow-up study.
papers.abstract: Alcohol use disorder (AUD) is a heterogeneous condition. Growing evidence highlights the role of the gut-brain axis in alcohol-related behaviors; however, longitudinal changes in the gut microbiome during pharmacological treatment for AUD remain poorly understood. In this 12-week study, we investigated gut microbiome composition and functional profiles in individuals with AUD undergoing naltrexone treatment. Seventy-two patients meeting DSM-5 criteria for AUD were enrolled. Stool samples were collected at baseline, week 4, and week 12 and analyzed using high-throughput 16 S rRNA gene followed
papers.summary: This study tracked gut microbiome changes over 12 weeks in people with alcohol use disorder taking naltrexone, finding that shifts in bacterial composition and function correlated with drinking outcomes. The results suggest the gut-brain axis may influence treatment response, highlighting the microbiome as a potential target for improving AUD therapies.

papers.title: Illuminating the "black boxes" of microbiome sequencing.
papers.abstract: Research on microbiomes is becoming common in undergraduate laboratory courses. These course-based undergraduate research experiences (CUREs) address many important microbiology and bioinformatics learning objectives related to science process skills. However, certain steps of the process for studying microbiomes represent "black boxes" for students. They never actually see any bacteria, but just extract bacterial DNA. Furthermore, how sequence data get translated into bacterial taxonomy tables is often opaque. We describe a protocol for evaluating communities of cultured bacteria that are seq
papers.summary: This paper describes a protocol for undergraduate lab courses that helps students understand the 'black boxes' of microbiome sequencing by evaluating communities of cultured bacteria. It addresses common learning gaps where students extract DNA but never see bacteria, and where sequence-to-taxonomy conversion remains opaque.

papers.title: Using sequence-derived microbiome information from bulk tank milk samples to assess the influence of post-milking teat disinfection in New Zealand dairy herds.
papers.abstract: Post-milking teat spraying plays an important role in the control of mastitis within New Zealand dairy herds. We examined the sample-level associations between post-milking teat spraying and bacterial levels in bulk tank milk (BTM) microbiomes, using shotgun DNA sequencing data within a non-experimental framework.
papers.summary: This study used shotgun DNA sequencing of bulk tank milk to investigate how post-milking teat disinfection practices affect the bacterial composition of milk in New Zealand dairy herds. The findings provide insights into the relationship between farm management practices and milk microbiome, which could inform mastitis control strategies.

papers.title: Grassland afforestation more than forestry intensification shapes soil multifunctionality via microbial compositional change under abiotic constraints.
papers.abstract: Soil ecosystem multifunctionality (EMF) is driven by the interplay of abiotic and biological factors, yet how these interactions respond to anthropogenic pressures remains poorly understood. Here, we evaluated how grassland afforestation and its intensification shape soil edaphic conditions, microbial diversity, and EMF along a 200 km grassland-eucalypt plantation transect in Argentina. EMF was estimated, accounting for six ecosystem functions related to nutrient provisioning, organic matter cycling, and pathogen control. Microbial diversity was studied through the taxonomic, functional, and p
papers.summary: This study shows that converting grasslands to eucalyptus plantations (afforestation) has a stronger negative impact on soil multifunctionality than simply intensifying existing forestry practices. The loss of soil function is driven by changes in microbial community composition, which are constrained by abiotic factors like soil conditions.

papers.title: Fermentative iron reduction by a psychrotolerant Clostridium-dominant consortium enriched from Antarctic penguin-impacted soils.
papers.abstract: Microbial iron cycling regulates nutrient availability and redox balance in global ecosystems, yet its pathways remain underexplored in ice-free Antarctic terrestrial ecosystems. This study reports the enrichment of a psychrotolerant microbial consortium from penguin-impacted soils on Beaufort Island, Antarctica, capable of reducing Fe(III) to Fe(II) at 4 °C via an anaerobic (likely fermentative) iron-reducing pathway. The consortium was dominated by Clostridium sensu stricto 13 and completely reduced 230 mg L-1 Fe(III) citrate within three months and drove the biogenic formation of magnetite
papers.summary: Researchers enriched a cold-tolerant microbial community from Antarctic penguin-impacted soils that reduces iron at 4°C through fermentation, dominated by Clostridium bacteria. The consortium completely converted iron within three months and produced the mineral magnetite, revealing a previously underappreciated microbial iron cycle in ice-free Antarctic soils.

papers.title: The combined effects of probiotic and high-intensity interval training on memory function in high fat diet-fed rats.
papers.abstract: This study aimed to investigate whether the probiotic Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG (LGG) (alone or combined with high-intensity interval training (HIIT)) could improve cognitive, electrophysiological changes, oxidative stress and metabolic parameters in HFD-fed rats.
papers.summary: This study found that combining the probiotic Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG with high-intensity interval training improved memory function in rats fed a high-fat diet, likely by reducing oxidative stress and improving metabolic parameters. The combination was more effective than either intervention alone.

papers.title: Bio-stimulants improve tomato growth by regulating the rhizosphere microbiome involved in phosphorus and nitrogen cycling.
papers.abstract: Bio-stimulants are promising environment friendly alternatives to support sustainable agricultural development, capable of boosting crop growth and yield while cutting down excessive dependence on chemical synthetic fertilizers. Nevertheless, the explicit regulatory mechanisms by which bio-stimulants exert the role of growth-promoting functions still remain largely unclear and require further systematic clarification. In this study, we explored the influences of bio-stimulants (rich in humic acid) on tomato growth performance and rhizosphere microbial community assembly via greenhouse trials,
papers.summary: This study shows that bio-stimulants rich in humic acid improve tomato growth by altering the rhizosphere microbiome, specifically enhancing microbial communities involved in phosphorus and nitrogen cycling. The findings provide mechanistic insight into how these environmentally friendly alternatives to chemical fertilizers promote plant growth.

papers.title: Temporal landscapes of the gut microbiota-host axis reveal mechanisms of age-related eggshell quality decline in laying hens.
papers.abstract: Age-related shifts in the gut microbiota of laying hens significantly affect eggshell quality. However, the temporal interactions of the gut microbiota during the eggshell mineralization cycle remain unclear. Existing research often overlooks the rhythmic synchronization required for mineralization, as well as the specific cellular landscape of the aging intestine that impairs effective host-microbe crosstalk. We integrated 16S rRNA sequencing, metagenomics, untargeted metabolomics, and single-cell RNA sequencing to compare young and aged hens during the initial (7 h post-oviposition) and rapi
papers.summary: This study combines 16S rRNA sequencing, metagenomics, metabolomics, and single-cell RNA sequencing to show that age-related shifts in the gut microbiota disrupt host-microbe crosstalk and eggshell mineralization in laying hens. The findings reveal that aging impairs rhythmic synchronization between gut microbes and host intestinal cells, leading to reduced eggshell quality.

papers.title: Viral modulation of sulfur-oxidizing bacteria drives organic carbon sink formation during primary succession in deglaciating ecosystems.
papers.abstract: Glacier forelands undergo a transition from oligotrophic to eutrophic conditions during primary succession. Reduced sulfur compounds may serve as an energy source for early microbial colonizers, yet the sulfur oxidation potential and key taxa remain largely unknown. Here, we perform a multi‑omics survey across a 130‑year chronosequence on the Tibetan Plateau. Glacial retreat profoundly reshapes both viral communities (61,394 viral operational taxonomic units, vOTUs) and microbial communities (404 metagenome‑assembled genomes, MAGs). Notably, Oxidative Dissimilatory sulfite reductase (Dsr) oper
papers.summary: This study shows that as glaciers retreat, viruses infecting sulfur-oxidizing bacteria help drive the formation of organic carbon sinks in newly exposed soils. By analyzing microbial and viral communities across a 130-year chronosequence, the researchers found that viral lysis of these bacteria releases organic matter that accumulates over time. This work reveals a previously unrecognized role for viruses in shaping carbon storage during primary succession in deglaciating ecosystems.

papers.title: Causal relationships between gut microbiota, C-reactive protein levels and colorectal cancer: A Mendelian randomization study.
papers.abstract: Gut microbiota have been associated with C-reactive protein (CRP) levels and colorectal cancer (CRC), but their causal relationships in humans remain unclear. We performed Mendelian randomization (MR) analyses to investigate causal relationships among gut microbiota, CRP, and CRC using genome-wide association studies (GWAS) summary data. The inverse variance weighted method was prespecified as the primary estimator, with complementary MR methods and sensitivity analyses used to assess robustness. Multiple-testing correction was applied across 209 gut microbial taxa. External validation and tar
papers.summary: This study uses Mendelian randomization to investigate causal links between gut microbiota, C-reactive protein (CRP) levels, and colorectal cancer (CRC). The findings suggest that certain gut microbial taxa may influence CRC risk, partly through effects on CRP levels, providing insights into potential causal pathways.

papers.title: Host-selective PMA-PCR enhances bacterial diversity recovery and lowers detection limits in plant microbiome profiling.
papers.abstract: Co-amplification of host DNA remains a significant obstacle in plant microbiome profiling, as universal taxonomic markers, such as the 16S rRNA gene, are also present in plant organellar genomes. While strategies like blocking primers can enrich bacterial reads, they rely on prior sequence knowledge and may introduce bias. We developed a sequence-independent approach that suppresses host DNA amplification by combining physical pretreatments that selectively compromise plant cell integrity with propidium monoazide (PMA) treatment, which binds exposed DNA and prevents its PCR amplification. We o
papers.summary: This paper presents a new method (host-selective PMA-PCR) that physically disrupts plant cells before adding a DNA-binding dye (PMA) to block host DNA amplification, thereby enriching bacterial sequences without needing prior sequence knowledge. The approach improves detection of low-abundance bacteria and recovers more diverse microbial communities from plant samples. It offers a simpler, less biased alternative to existing host DNA suppression techniques for plant microbiome studies.

papers.title: The Kenyan Human Gut Virome Catalogue reveals extensive viral diversity and age-dependent community structure.
papers.abstract: The human gut virome is a critical yet understudied component of the microbiome that shapes microbial community structure and host-microbe interactions. However, most existing human gut virome reference databases have been constructed predominantly from populations in high-income countries, resulting in the substantial underrepresentation of African populations. To help address this disparity, we developed the Kenyan Human Gut Virome Catalogue (KHGVC), the first comprehensive human gut virome resource for Kenya and the first country-specific human gut virome catalogue from Africa. Using a stan
papers.summary: Researchers created the first comprehensive human gut virome catalogue for Kenya, addressing the underrepresentation of African populations in existing databases. The catalogue reveals extensive viral diversity and shows that the gut virome community structure changes with age.

papers.title: Bioinformatics roadmap for characterizing the gut microbiome to study its interactions and associations with the gut mucosal immune system.
papers.abstract: Cataloging the gut microbiome and understanding its interactions and association with the host immune system remains relevant to identify potential treatments for complex chronic diseases, including cancer. Studying the gut microbiome is more accessible than ever, thanks to the dramatic cost reduction of sequencing and the continuous improvements in bioinformatics and machine learning approaches. A plethora of bioinformatics and statistical methods are currently available to analyze gut microbiome sequencing data and evaluate its interactions with the host immune system across health and disea
papers.summary: This review provides a practical roadmap for using bioinformatics and machine learning to analyze gut microbiome sequencing data and study its interactions with the host mucosal immune system. It highlights how cost-effective sequencing and improved computational methods now make it easier to investigate microbiome-immune relationships in chronic diseases like cancer. The paper serves as a guide for researchers navigating the available tools and statistical approaches in this field.

papers.title: Microbiome analysis of Cystic Fibrosis sputum presents higher sensitivity than the conventional bacterial culture.
papers.abstract: Microbiological communities in the airway of Cystic Fibrosis (CF) patients may be associated with clinical conditions and bacterial exacerbation. The main aim of this study was to establish the correlation between the airway microbiome and the bacteriological culture. We also correlated the microbiome data with the CFTR mutation, presence/absence of leukocytes and hospitalization status of CF patients.
papers.journal: The Brazilian journal of infectious diseases : an official publication of the Brazilian Society of Infectious Diseases
papers.summary: This study shows that analyzing the airway microbiome of cystic fibrosis patients using DNA sequencing detects more bacterial species than standard culture methods, and that microbiome data correlates with clinical factors like CFTR mutation and hospitalization status.

papers.title: VirBinn improves viral genome binning from metagenomic Hi-C through graph diffusion.
papers.abstract: Metagenomic Hi-C provides in situ proximity signals that can improve genome binning and enable virus-host-association analysis. However, viral genome recovery remains difficult because virus-virus Hi-C contact matrices are extremely sparse. Viral genomes are small, often low-abundance, and frequently assemble into short contigs, leaving many true within-genome links unobserved and causing viral bins to fragment.
papers.summary: VirBinn is a new computational tool that uses graph diffusion to improve the recovery of viral genomes from metagenomic Hi-C data, which is challenging due to sparse contact signals. By enhancing within-genome connectivity, it reduces fragmentation of viral bins and improves binning accuracy.

papers.title: How Host Phylogeny, Diet, and Habitat Affect Gut Microbial Diversity in Wild Snakes.
papers.abstract: Gut microbiota plays critical roles in host digestion, immune regulation, neurochemical signaling, and metabolic homeostasis. Based on wild snakes (73 individuals from 23 species) from China, we explored the composition, characteristics, and functions of gut microbes across different groups using fecal metagenomic samples; further we explored the relative contributions of host phylogeny, diet, and habitat to the microbial structure. Among 23 wild snake species, the dominant gut microbial phyla were Proteobacteria, Bacteroidetes, Firmicutes, and Actinobacteria, with Bacteroides, Salmonella, Cit
papers.summary: This study analyzed fecal metagenomes from 73 wild snakes across 23 species in China to characterize gut microbial composition and assess the relative influence of host phylogeny, diet, and habitat. The dominant bacterial phyla were Proteobacteria, Bacteroidetes, Firmicutes, and Actinobacteria, and the findings highlight how ecological and evolutionary factors shape the snake gut microbiome.

papers.title: Alterations in the fecal virome and bacteriome-virome interplay in IPAH.
papers.abstract: Idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension (IPAH) is a life-threatening cardiovascular disorder characterized by complex multisystem disturbances. Although alterations in the gut microbiota have been reported in IPAH, how the gut virome interacts with bacterial communities and host metabolism remains unclear.
papers.summary: This study investigates changes in the gut virome (viral community) and its interactions with bacteria in patients with idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension (IPAH), a severe cardiovascular disease. The findings reveal distinct alterations in the fecal virome and disrupted bacteriome-virome interplay, suggesting that viral communities may contribute to the disease's pathophysiology.

papers.title: Comparison of library preparation protocols and bioinformatic pipelines in high-throughput 16S rRNA gene sequencing.
papers.abstract: 16S rRNA gene sequencing is widely used for bacterial community profiling in both clinical and research contexts. The expanding availability of library preparation protocols and bioinformatic pipelines increases analytical flexibility but may also introduce method-dependent biases that affect inferred microbial composition and relative abundance estimates. The relative impact of library preparation protocol, amplicon region, and bioinformatic pipeline on species-level taxonomic inference and compositional agreement remains insufficiently characterised. We therefore compared the Illumina 16S Me
papers.summary: This study systematically compared different library preparation protocols and bioinformatic pipelines for 16S rRNA gene sequencing, finding that both steps introduce significant biases that affect bacterial community profiling. The choice of protocol and pipeline can substantially alter inferred microbial composition and relative abundance estimates, highlighting the need for careful method selection and standardization in microbiome studies.

papers.title: Qualitative profiling of the gut-specific chlamydial population in Ixodes ricinus ticks.
papers.abstract: Members of the phylum Chlamydiota are obligate intracellular bacteria increasingly recognized across a wide range of arthropod hosts, including ticks. In this study, we investigated the diversity and distribution of chlamydiae in Ixodes ricinus ticks and their potential association with Lyme borreliosis spirochetes. A total of 250 questing nymphal and female I. ricinus ticks were collected from three recreational sites in Vienna, Austria. Individual tick guts were screened for chlamydiae using pan-Chlamydiota PCR assays targeting the 16S rRNA gene, followed by sequencing for taxonomic identifi
papers.summary: This study investigates the diversity of Chlamydiota bacteria in the guts of Ixodes ricinus ticks from Austria, finding that a significant proportion of ticks carry these bacteria, with some species potentially associated with Lyme disease spirochetes. The research provides new insights into the tick microbiome and its possible role in pathogen transmission.

papers.title: Mapping potential pathogen profiling in cetacean blow: comparative insights from sequencing technologies.
papers.abstract: Cetaceans play a critical role in marine ecosystems and function as sentinel species for detecting environmental perturbations, underscoring the importance of assessing their health for effective marine conservation. This study employed 16S rRNA gene sequencing to characterize the prokaryotic communities present in exhaled breath condensate (EBC) samples from cetaceans, utilizing both short-read (Illumina) and long-read (PacBio) sequencing platforms. Putative pathogenic taxa were identified using the Multiple Bacterial Pathogen Detection (MBPD) database. Substantial differences in microbial co
papers.summary: This study used 16S rRNA gene sequencing on both short-read (Illumina) and long-read (PacBio) platforms to characterize bacterial communities in cetacean exhaled breath condensate, identifying potential pathogens via a specialized database. The findings reveal significant differences in microbial composition between sequencing technologies, highlighting the importance of platform choice for accurate pathogen profiling in marine mammal health monitoring.

papers.title: A novel transformer model of protein domains for viral taxonomy classification.
papers.abstract: Viruses with carefully curated taxonomic assignments (such as those in the ICTV taxonomy) still represent only a small fraction of viruses identified through sequencing data from virome or microbiome projects. It is therefore critical to develop methods that can assign viruses at multiple taxonomic ranks, so that a virus deemed novel at a given rank may still be placed into a higher-level taxon. Sequence-similarity-based approaches can classify viruses that share substantial genomic similarity with known viruses (e.g. those belonging to the same species or genus); however, their performance dr
papers.summary: This paper presents a novel transformer model that uses protein domain information to classify viruses into taxonomic ranks, even when they are novel at lower levels. The approach addresses the limitation of sequence-similarity methods, which struggle with highly divergent viruses, by leveraging domain architectures for broader taxonomic placement.

papers.title: Novel Distance Regression for Repeated Outcomes With Missing Data: Applications to Longitudinal and Crossover Studies of Microbiome Beta-Diversity.
papers.abstract: The human microbiome plays a crucial role in health, but understanding its dynamic relationship with the host requires regular monitoring. Beyond challenges such as high dimensionality and sparsity, additional complexities arise, particularly within-cluster correlation from repeated measures and pervasive missing data. To address these issues, we develop Edger, a novel distance regression method for modeling community-level beta-diversity dynamics and their interactions with treatment or host physiology. By focusing on beta-diversity, a distance metric between microbial profiles, Edger (Ensemb
papers.summary: This paper introduces Edger, a new statistical method for analyzing microbiome beta-diversity in studies with repeated measures and missing data. Edger handles within-cluster correlations and missing values better than existing approaches, enabling more reliable analysis of how microbial communities change over time in response to treatments or host factors.

papers.title: Multi-Omics Profiling of the Scaphoideus titanus Yeast-Like Symbiont Guides the Bioinformatic Discovery of Related Fungal Symbioses in Insects.
papers.abstract: Symbiotic partnerships have opened new ecological niches and contributed to the remarkable diversification of insects. The leafhopper Scaphoideus titanus, a phloem-feeding insect known to be the primary vector of Flavescence dorée phytoplasma, harbours two primary endosymbionts: the bacterium 'Candidatus Karelsulcia muelleri' and a yeast-like symbiont (YLS). While most studies on insect-associated microorganisms have focused on obligate bacterial symbionts, fungal endosymbionts, although documented for almost a century, are only now gaining renewed attention for their evolutionary and ecologic
papers.summary: This study profiles the yeast-like symbiont (YLS) of the leafhopper Scaphoideus titanus using multi-omics, revealing its metabolic capabilities and evolutionary role. The findings provide a bioinformatic framework to discover related fungal symbioses in other insects, highlighting the understudied importance of fungal endosymbionts in insect evolution and ecology.

papers.title: High postprandial endotoxemia is associated with recurrence of cardiovascular events in patients with coronary heart disease: from the CORDIOPREV randomized clinical trial.
papers.abstract: The translocation into the systemic circulation of proinflammatory bacterial components such as lipopolysaccharide (LPS) has been linked to cardiovascular disease (CVD).
papers.journal: The American journal of clinical nutrition
papers.summary: This study found that high levels of bacterial endotoxins in the blood after meals are linked to a higher risk of recurrent cardiovascular events in patients with coronary heart disease. The findings suggest that post-meal endotoxemia could be a target for dietary interventions to reduce cardiovascular risk.

papers.title: Suggestive associations between genetically predicted gut microbiota and endometriosis: a two-sample Mendelian randomization study.
papers.abstract: Introduction. Endometriosis affects 10-20% of reproductive-age women. Emerging evidence links the gut microbiota to endometriosis pathogenesis, but observational studies are limited by confounding, reverse causation and uncertainty about whether reported microbial signals represent reproducible aetiological associations.Hypothesis/Gap Statement. Whether genetically predicted gut microbial genera are associated with endometriosis risk remains unclear, and available observational evidence does not establish robust causal effects after accounting for multiple testing.Aim. This study aimed to expl
papers.summary: This study used genetic data to investigate whether gut bacteria can causally influence endometriosis risk. The authors found suggestive associations between several gut microbial genera and endometriosis, but these did not survive strict multiple-testing correction, meaning the evidence for a causal link remains weak. The work highlights the need for larger studies to clarify whether the gut microbiome plays a role in endometriosis development.

papers.title: High-throughput Profiling of Pseudouridines in Microbiome-derived Bacterial RNA.
papers.abstract: Pseudouridine (Ψ) is a widespread RNA modification that influences RNA stability, structure, and translation. However, its role in bacterial mRNA, particularly within complex microbiomes, remains poorly defined. Here, we describe a bisulfite-based sequencing workflow coupled with a scalable computational pipeline for base-resolution, quantitative mapping of pseudouridine in microbiome transcriptomes. The protocol is optimized for low-input, high-complexity samples and includes strategies for efficient RNA extraction, ribosomal RNA depletion, bisulfite conversion, library preparation, and seque
papers.summary: This paper presents a new method for mapping pseudouridine modifications in bacterial RNA from complex microbiomes. The workflow combines bisulfite sequencing with a computational pipeline to achieve base-resolution, quantitative mapping from low-input samples.

papers.title: Deciphering soybean-microbiome interactions: from rhizosphere dynamics to sustainable yield enhancement.
papers.abstract: The soybean plant (Glycine max L.) is an important crop for valuable food source because of its high levels of protein and oil, thus contributing greatly to a sustainable system for producing food through biological nitrogen fixation. Recent research supports the theory that the soybean-associated microbiome located in the rhizosphere is a crucial regulatory mechanism governing plant growth, nutrient acquisition, and stress tolerance. Additionally, advances in metagenomics, metatranscriptomics, metabolomics, and root exudate profiling via LC‒MS have shown that soybean roots alter the microbial
papers.summary: This review discusses how the soybean-associated microbiome in the rhizosphere regulates plant growth, nutrient uptake, and stress tolerance, with recent multi-omics approaches revealing that root exudates shape microbial communities. Understanding these interactions could lead to strategies for sustainable yield enhancement in soybean, an important crop for protein and oil production.

papers.title: Genome-scale metabolic models predict diet- and lifestyle-driven shifts of ecological interactions in the gut microbiome.
papers.abstract: Microbiomes and their host environments form complex, interconnected ecosystems. The microbial species within a microbiome, on the one hand, compete for resources, while on the other hand, they exchange vital metabolites to support their survival. These interactions are influenced by the microbial genetic repertoire, environmental conditions, and availability of nutrients. We developed EcoGS (http://www.github.com/KaletaLab/EcoGS), a metabolic modeling tool designed to predict the ecological interactions between pairs of microbes. Applying EcoGS to the microbiomes of two distinct human cohorts
papers.summary: This study introduces EcoGS, a metabolic modeling tool that predicts how diet and lifestyle affect ecological interactions (like competition and cooperation) between gut microbes. By applying it to human gut microbiome data, the tool reveals that shifts in nutrient availability can fundamentally alter microbial community dynamics.

papers.title: Long-read whole-genome sequencing dataset of microbial communities from industrially and municipally impacted freshwater wetlands in South Africa.
papers.abstract: This article describes a long-read whole-genome shotgun sequencing dataset generated from microbial communities inhabiting industrially and municipally impacted freshwater wetlands in South Africa. Surface water samples were collected from five strategically selected sites exposed to distinct anthropogenic pressures, including industrial effluent discharge, sewage overflow, greywater inputs, informal settlement runoff, and landfill leachate to generate a unique microbial genomic data. Environmental DNA was extracted and sequenced using the PacBio Sequel IIe platform, producing high-fidelity lo
papers.summary: This paper presents a long-read whole-genome sequencing dataset of microbial communities from South African freshwater wetlands impacted by industrial and municipal pollution. The dataset, generated using PacBio sequencing, provides a genomic resource for studying microbial responses to various anthropogenic contaminants.

papers.title: PacBio HiFi sequencing datasets of culture-enriched airborne microbial cave communities from dolomitic Sudwala Caves, South Africa.
papers.abstract: We present a dataset integrating physico-chemical air quality measurements with long-read PacBio HiFi shotgun metagenomic sequences from culture-enriched airborne samples collected in Sudwala Caves, one of the oldest known cave systems in South Africa. This resource provides baseline characterization of airborne microbial communities and associated environmental parameters within a subterranean karst ecosystem. A total of 106 air samples were collected across six different cave compartments and three external reference sites spanning two seasonal periods, the winter-spring transition (Septembe
papers.summary: This paper presents a new dataset combining air quality measurements with long-read PacBio HiFi metagenomic sequencing of airborne microbes from the Sudwala Caves in South Africa. The resource characterizes airborne microbial communities across different cave compartments and seasons, providing a baseline for understanding microbial life in subterranean karst ecosystems.

papers.title: Rhizospheric Microbes and Nanoparticles Synergize to Enhance Plant Immune Responses.
papers.abstract: Sustainable crop production increasingly requires innovative strategies that can enhance plant resilience while reducing dependence on synthetic agrochemicals. Recent advances in nanotechnology and rhizosphere microbiome research have created new opportunities to strengthen plant defense systems through integrated biological and material-based approaches. This review introduces the concept of nanobiotic synergies, defined as the strategic combination of engineered or biologically synthesized nanoparticles with beneficial rhizospheric microorganisms to improve plant immunity, stress tolerance,
papers.summary: This review proposes the concept of 'nanobiotic synergies,' where combining nanoparticles with beneficial soil microbes can boost plant immune responses and stress tolerance, offering a sustainable alternative to synthetic agrochemicals. It highlights how recent advances in nanotechnology and microbiome research can be integrated to strengthen plant defense systems.

papers.title: 16S rRNA gene sequencing dataset describing the diversity and structure of soil bacterial communities across four pesticide-free agroecological cropping systems of arable crops from the CA-SYS experiment between 2018 and 2021.
papers.abstract: A dataset describing soil bacterial community diversity and structure was generated from the CA-SYS experimental platform (INRAE, France), a long-term research facility designed to evaluate agroecological practices under contrasting soil management without pesticide use. Soil samples were collected from 42 experimental plots (4 subplots per plot) across four cropping systems, including no-till and tilled systems with or without nitrogen inputs, over four sampling years (2018-2021). It comprises 640 samples for which sequencing data were obtained, and 595 samples after quality control, represen
papers.summary: This dataset provides 16S rRNA gene sequencing data from soil bacterial communities in four pesticide-free agroecological cropping systems over four years. It includes 595 high-quality samples from 42 plots with varying tillage and nitrogen inputs, offering a resource for studying soil microbiome responses to sustainable farming practices.

papers.title: Metagenome-assembled genomes from the temperate forest phyllosphere in Eastern Canada.
papers.abstract: The phyllosphere is host to diverse microbial communities surviving in dynamic environmental conditions and which form important relationships with their hosts. Here, we constructed metagenome-assembled genomes (MAGs) from 25 temperate forest phyllosphere samples collected in Eastern Canada. We found 423 dereplicated MAGs with completeness ≥50% and contamination ≤10%, using a combination of co-assembly strategies. The MAGs were predominantly classified into the bacterial phyla Pseudomonadota (n=197), Actinomycetota (n=88) and Acidobacteriota (n=50) and included two archaeal MAGs in the phylum
papers.summary: Researchers constructed 423 metagenome-assembled genomes (MAGs) from microbes living on tree leaves (phyllosphere) in Eastern Canadian temperate forests. The MAGs were mostly bacteria from three major phyla, with two archaeal genomes also recovered, providing a new genomic resource for understanding plant-microbe interactions in forest ecosystems.

papers.title: Microbial dysbiosis and inferred functional profiling reveals the potential role of Methylobacterium in prostate cancer.
papers.abstract: Prostate cancer (PCa) is a leading malignancy in men, with a multifactorial aetiology involving genetic, hormonal, and microbial factors. Although emerging evidence implicates tumour-associated microbial communities in cancer biology, microbial signatures in PCa, particularly in Arab populations, remain underexplored. This study aimed to characterize the prostate tissue microbiota in an Arab cohort and explore associations with clinical features.
papers.journal: Frontiers in cellular and infection microbiology
papers.summary: This study characterizes the prostate tissue microbiome in an Arab cohort with prostate cancer, finding that microbial dysbiosis and functional profiling point to a potential role for Methylobacterium in the disease. The research addresses a gap in understanding tumor-associated microbial signatures in understudied populations.

papers.title: DeepTaxa: a hybrid CNN-BERT framework for 16S rRNA taxonomic classification.
papers.abstract: Accurate species-level classification of prokaryotic 16S rRNA sequences remains difficult: existing tools rely on exact alignment, k-mer heuristics, or phylogenetic placement and are limited by incomplete reference databases. Deep learning approaches in microbial genomics have focused largely on whole-genome metagenomics, leaving 16S taxonomy under-supported.
papers.summary: DeepTaxa introduces a hybrid CNN-BERT model that improves species-level classification of 16S rRNA sequences by learning sequence features directly, overcoming limitations of alignment-based and k-mer methods. This deep learning approach fills a gap in 16S taxonomy tools, offering more accurate classification even with incomplete reference databases.

papers.title: Exploring the Tenebrio molitor gut microbiota response to LDPE and PET: putative genetic indicators and methodological insights.
papers.abstract: Insect gut microbiomes are recognized as potential reservoirs of enzymatic activities relevant to plastic metabolism. Here, we investigated the taxonomic and functional dynamics of the Tenebrio molitor gut microbiota under dietary exposure to low-density polyethylene (LDPE) and polyethylene terephthalate (PET) using 16S rRNA sequencing and shotgun metagenomics. Significant compositional shifts were detected at the ASV level, with plastic-fed cohorts showing enrichment of taxa implicated in xenobiotic metabolism. Predicted functional changes suggested altered abundance of pathways related to ar
papers.summary: This study examines how the gut microbiome of mealworms (Tenebrio molitor) changes when fed low-density polyethylene (LDPE) and polyethylene terephthalate (PET) plastics. Using DNA sequencing, the researchers found that plastic diets shift the microbial community composition and enrich bacteria with genes potentially involved in breaking down these synthetic materials.

papers.title: Revealing the bacterial diversity and variation of white filamentous microbial mats in marine mangroves of Guadeloupe Island in relation to human activities.
papers.abstract: White filamentous microbial mats are complex benthic communities, typically structured by sulfur-oxidizing bacteria from the Beggiatoaceae family, yet their diversity and ecological responses in mangrove ecosystems remain poorly characterized. Here, we provide a high-resolution analysis of bacterial communities associated with white microbial mats in marine mangrove sediments of Guadeloupe using 16S rRNA metabarcoding. Bacterial community composition was compared across sites with different levels of anthropogenic impact (protected, natural, and urban). While overall diversity remained stable,
papers.summary: This study uses 16S rRNA metabarcoding to characterize bacterial communities in white filamentous microbial mats from Guadeloupe mangroves, finding that overall diversity remains stable across sites with different human impact levels. The research provides a high-resolution analysis of these poorly understood communities, revealing how anthropogenic activities may influence specific bacterial groups.

papers.title: Metagenomic characterization of gut microbiota in rheumatoid arthritis-associated interstitial lung disease: taxonomic shifts and clinical correlations.
papers.abstract: Rheumatoid arthritis-associated interstitial lung disease (RA-ILD) is a severe extra-articular manifestation with limited diagnostic biomarkers. While gut microbiota dysbiosis contributes to rheumatoid arthritis (RA) pathogenesis, its specific role in RA-ILD remains poorly characterized.
papers.summary: This study characterizes the gut microbiome in patients with rheumatoid arthritis-associated interstitial lung disease (RA-ILD), identifying specific taxonomic shifts compared to RA patients without lung disease. These microbial changes correlate with clinical parameters, suggesting that gut microbiota composition may serve as a diagnostic biomarker for RA-ILD.

papers.title: Integrated multi-omics analysis reveals distinct microbiota-metabolite signatures and a novel HCN2-2-hydroxybutyric acid interaction in inflammatory bowel disease.
papers.abstract: Gut microbiota-derived short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs) exert critical regulatory functions in inflammatory bowel disease (IBD). However, integrated profiling of fecal SCFA signatures alongside gut microbiota composition in ulcerative colitis (UC) and Crohn's disease (CD) remains insufficiently characterized. Furthermore, the molecular mechanisms through which microbiota metabolites engage host protein targets warrant systematic investigation.
papers.summary: This study profiles fecal short-chain fatty acids and gut microbiota in ulcerative colitis and Crohn's disease, identifying distinct metabolite-microbe signatures for each subtype. It further uncovers a novel interaction between the ion channel HCN2 and 2-hydroxybutyric acid, suggesting a potential molecular mechanism by which microbial metabolites influence host physiology in inflammatory bowel disease.

papers.title: Key targets and mechanisms by which gut microbiota-derived metabolites regulate Alzheimer's disease through the immune - inflammatory pathway: Based on network pharmacology and molecular docking.
papers.abstract: This study integrated network pharmacology, bioinformatics, and molecular docking to explore potential immune-inflammatory pathways associated with the relationship between gut microbiota-derived metabolites and Alzheimer's disease (AD). A total of 260 gut microbiota - derived metabolites were initially retrieved, and 196 common targets were identified by intersecting predicted metabolite-associated targets with AD-related targets. Further screening identified 14 key overlapping targets, including IL6, NFKB1, IL1B, PTGS2, TLR4, and PPARG. Protein-protein interaction (PPI) network analysis iden
papers.summary: This study uses network pharmacology and molecular docking to identify gut microbiota-derived metabolites and their key targets (e.g., IL6, NFKB1, TLR4) that may regulate Alzheimer's disease through immune-inflammatory pathways. The findings suggest specific metabolites and pathways that could be targeted for therapeutic intervention.

papers.title: Vaginal Acinetobacter infection as a cause of cervical collagenolysis.
papers.abstract: The vaginal microbiota demonstrates considerable heterogeneity and is shaped by factors such as race, geographic location, and individual variation. Although its central role in maintaining host health is well established, the precise mechanisms through which it contributes to the onset and progression of preterm birth remain incompletely understood and continue to be debated.
papers.journal: Frontiers in cellular and infection microbiology
papers.summary: This paper investigates how vaginal Acinetobacter infection may contribute to preterm birth by breaking down cervical collagen. The study suggests that specific microbial infections can directly weaken cervical tissue, offering a potential mechanism linking vaginal microbiota to preterm labor.

papers.title: Microbiome-Metabolome Crosstalk in HPV Pathogenesis: From Ecosystem Dynamics to Translational Biomarkers.
papers.abstract: High-risk human papillomavirus (hrHPV) infection alters the cervicovaginal microenvironment, driving metabolic reprogramming that influences viral persistence and progression to cervical cancer. This review adopts a systems-level perspective to synthesize findings from recent metabolomic studies across urine, vaginal swabs, and cervicovaginal fluids, highlighting consistent trends from cervicovaginal health through hrHPV infection, persistence, cervical lesion development, and cancer. HPV infection is characterized by increased microbial amines and oxidative stress, whereas viral persistence a
papers.summary: This review synthesizes metabolomic studies to show how high-risk HPV infection alters the cervicovaginal microenvironment through metabolic reprogramming, with distinct metabolic signatures (increased microbial amines and oxidative stress) associated with different stages of infection, persistence, and progression to cervical cancer.

papers.title: A pilot randomized trial on the usability and acceptability of an app (MyIBDDiet) to improve the self-management of anti-inflammatory diet for individuals with inflammatory bowel disease: A protocol paper.
papers.abstract: The role of diet in the management of inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) is increasingly recognized with recent guidelines providing specific dietary recommendations. Although mobile health apps targeting diet and lifestyle habits in IBD are emerging, few are designed for self-management or have been formally evaluated for effectiveness. We have co-designed a diet guidance and tracking app (MyIBDDiet) with and for patients with IBD with the aim of improving overall diet profile. We will be conducting a 60-day single-centre pilot randomized trial of 40 IBD patients randomized in 1:1 ratio to MyIB
papers.summary: This protocol describes a pilot trial of a co-designed mobile app (MyIBDDiet) to help patients with inflammatory bowel disease self-manage an anti-inflammatory diet. The study will randomize 40 patients to test the app's usability and acceptability over 60 days, addressing a gap in formally evaluated digital tools for IBD dietary management.

papers.title: Assessment of the bacterial community of the human upper respiratory tract in patients affected by Covid-19.
papers.abstract: Given the challenges posed by the 2019 coronavirus disease (COVID-19), understanding the role of the microbiota is crucial. The analysis of this microbial community in the body not only expands our understanding of health, but also provides insights into the interaction between viruses, microbiota, and the human host. In this study, we aimed to identify possible variations in bacterial diversity caused by Severe Acute Respiratory Syndrome Coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2). The study involved 45 volunteers, divided into the Positive Group (PG) consisting of 14 patients and the Negative Group (NG) cons
papers.summary: This study analyzed the bacterial community in the upper respiratory tract of COVID-19 patients and healthy controls, finding that SARS-CoV-2 infection is associated with changes in bacterial diversity. The results suggest that COVID-19 may alter the respiratory microbiota, which could influence disease progression and host-virus interactions.
